data.title: Kansas City Life Insurance reports Q2 results
data.description: • Kansas City Life Insurance press release (OTC:KCLI): Q2 net loss of $28.1 million or $2.90 per share.

data.title: DIH receives deficiency letter from Nasdaq
data.description:
• DIH (NASDAQ:DHAI) on Friday said it received a Nasdaq notice, stating that the company was not in compliance with Nasdaq Listing Rule as it had not filed its Annual Report on Form 10-K for the period ended March 31, 2025, with the Securities and Exchange Commission.
  • Under Nasdaq rules, the company has 60 calendar days from the date of the notice to file its Annual Report on Form 10-K for the period ended March 31, 2025. If the company is unable to file the Annual Report by that date, the company is permitted to submit a plan of compliance on or prior to that date. 
  • The notice has no immediate impact on the listing or trading of the Company’s securities on Nasdaq.
  • DHAI -19.77% after hours to $0.2.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Starbucks files mixed shelf offerings; size not disclosed
data.description: • Starbucks may offer debt, common stock, preferred stock, warrants, subscription rights, purchase contracts, and units.

data.title: Sagen MI Canada reports Q2 results
data.description:
• Sagen MI Canada  press release (OTC:SGMCF): Q2 Net income of $111 million was $40 million lower than the same quarter in the prior year. 
  • The company also announced today that its Board of Directors had declared a dividend of $0.3375 per Class A preferred share, Series 1, payable on September 29, 2025, to holders of record at the close of business on September 15, 2025.

data.title: Andlauer Healthcare reports Q2 results
data.description:
• Andlauer Healthcare press release (OTCPK:ANDHF): Q2 Revenue of C$156.6M beats by C$33.77M.
  • Operating income was C$18.9 million.
  • Net income was C$13.5 million.
  • EBITDA totaled C$38.1 million.
  • EBITDA Margin was 24.3%.
  • EPS of C$0.34

data.title: Smart Share Global enters into definitive merger agreement for going private transaction
data.description:
• Smart Share Global (NASDAQ:EM) has entered into a definitive agreement and plan of merger with Mobile Charging Group, Mobile Charging Investment and Mobile Charging Merger in a transaction implying an equity value of the company of about $327 million in which the company will be acquired by a consortium of investors.
  • The consortium includes Trustar Mobile Charging; Mars Guangyuan Cai, chairman of the board and CEO of the company;  Peifeng Xu, director and president of the company; Victor Yaoyu Zhang, chief marketing officer and Maria Yi Xin, director and chief financial officer of the company.
  • The merger consideration represents a premium of 74.8% to the closing trading price of the ADSs on January 3, 2025, the last trading day prior to the company’s receipt of the going private proposal.
  • The consortium intends to fund the merger through a combination of cash contributions from certain members to their respective equity commitment letters, proceeds from certain committed term loan facility to be provided by Bank of China, Shanghai Branch, and rollover equity contributions by the Rollover Shareholders.
  • The merger is currently expected to close during the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Gerdau signals post-2026 CapEx reduction and prioritizes North America amid Brazil import challenges
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Gerdau S.A. (GGB) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gustavo Werneck Da Cunha highlighted a record low in workplace accident rates and noted, "we ended the second quarter of '25 with another positive milestone in our historical series of workplace accident rates, reinforcing our commitment to the health, well-being and safety of our employees and all stakeholders with whom we interact."
  • Werneck emphasized the "strong recovery of our North American operations, which in the second quarter posted the highest all-time share in our results, representing 61% of consolidated EBITDA."
  • He addressed the ongoing challenges in Brazil, stating, "the domestic market continued to be impacted by excessive imports of steel throughout the second quarter," and announced a strategic shift: "Given this scenario...we decided to reduce our investments in Brazil and the details of which we will announce over the coming months."
  • CFO Rafael Dorneles Japur reported, "Our adjusted EBITDA was BRL 2.6 billion, 6.6% higher than in the first quarter of 2025, with the North America segment's performance standing out...Net income stood at BRL 864 million or BRL 0.43 per share, up 14% compared to the first quarter of '25."
  • Japur also detailed capital structure moves: "During this first quarter, we issued 2 important debt issuance, a USD 650 million bond maturing in 2035 and a 7-year debenture totaling BRL 1.4 billion."
  • CapEx investment was highlighted by Japur: "We invested BRL 1.6 billion in the quarter, where we allocated most of it in our Miguel Burnier sustainable mining project, which has already reached 72% completion."

Outlook

  • Werneck indicated, "for the coming months, we see a resilient civil construction market," but warned of ongoing risks from high import levels in Brazil and closely monitored impacts on the automotive and agricultural sectors.
  • The CEO confirmed a strategic reduction: "a decision has been made to reduce the global CapEx disbursement from 2026 and beyond...after 2026, a decision has been made to reduce the level of disbursements."
  • Japur reiterated that "the BRL 6 billion we announced of disbursement for this year will be fully executed. But after 2026, a decision has been made to reduce the level of disbursements."
  • The company expects "an important part of this BRL 1.1 billion will start being captured along 2026" from the Miguel Burnier project.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBITDA reached BRL 2.6 billion, up 6.6% quarter-over-quarter, with North America contributing 61% of consolidated EBITDA.
  • Net income was BRL 864 million or BRL 0.43 per share, a 14% increase from Q1 2025.
  • CapEx for the quarter totaled BRL 1.6 billion, with the majority allocated to the Miguel Burnier mining project.
  • The company approved a dividend distribution of BRL 0.12 per share, totaling BRL 239 million, and continued its 2025 share buyback program, achieving 68% completion representing 2.2% of outstanding shares.
  • The payout ratio for the quarter was 90%.
  • Leverage ratio ended the period at 0.85x net debt over EBITDA.

Q&A

  • Lucas Laghi, XP: Asked about CapEx expectations and capital allocation amid differing market contexts. Werneck explained the decision to reduce CapEx post-2026, prioritizing North American investments, while Japur provided assumptions for the Miguel Burnier project, highlighting its expected BRL 1.1 billion incremental EBITDA.
  • Leonardo Correa, BTG Pactual: Questioned net debt increase and market strategy for rebar pricing. Japur attributed leverage growth to shareholder returns and ongoing CapEx, while Werneck emphasized maintaining market share and acknowledged challenges from imports, noting, "profitability, margins and prices are below what they should be."
  • Daniel Sasson, Itau BBA: Inquired about gross debt policy and structural measures. Werneck explained cash position nuances due to regional differences and confirmed 1,500 dismissals in 2025, with ongoing reviews of structural responses to Brazil's competitive pressures.
  • Rafael Barcellos, Bradesco BBI: Probed the rebar market and CapEx balance. Japur stated, "it makes no sense for us to give out any market share," and clarified that maintenance CapEx would continue even as growth investments in Brazil are reduced.
  • Rodolfo R. De Angele, JPMorgan: Sought details on cost reduction in Brazil and U.S. strategy. Japur outlined cost dilution opportunities as production normalizes, while Werneck described a strong U.S. demand pipeline in sectors like solar and data centers and noted the strategic postponement of downtime at the Midlothian facility to meet solid demand.
  • Gabriel Barra, Citi: Asked about capital allocation with higher expected cash generation. Werneck prioritized share buybacks, stating, "our #1 priority in the current context would be to have share buybacks," and anticipated significant EBITDA growth from maturing projects.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a critical and probing tone, pressing management on capital allocation, leverage, and market competitiveness, particularly regarding Brazil’s exposure to imports and the rationale for debt and payout levels.
  • Management was confident yet defensive, especially when addressing Brazil’s challenges, using phrases like "we understand that this is something temporary" and "the decision has been made."
  • Compared to last quarter, analyst skepticism on Brazil’s prospects increased, while management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to firmer strategic realignment toward North America.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most significant change was the explicit announcement of post-2026 CapEx reduction, focused on Brazil, compared to prior guidance that only hinted at potential lower future disbursements.
  • North America’s contribution to consolidated EBITDA reached a record 61%, versus last quarter’s noted recovery.
  • Brazil’s import pressures intensified, leading to concrete strategic shifts, whereas previously management awaited governmental action.
  • Analysts shifted focus from general capital allocation to more pointed questions on leverage, cost structure, and the sustainability of dividends and buybacks.
  • Management’s confidence in North America increased, with more detail on backlog strength and sector opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Persistent high steel imports in Brazil continue to erode domestic profitability and threaten future investments.
  • Management is monitoring potential impacts from U.S. tariffs on Brazil and the risk of further import surges.
  • Short-term cost inflation and production disruptions in Brazil, tied to ongoing CapEx projects, were identified but are expected to ease as projects ramp up.
  • Analysts raised concerns related to elevated debt, cash generation, and the sustainability of high payout ratios amid adverse Brazilian market dynamics.

Final Takeaway

Management reinforced that Gerdau’s resilience in Q2 2025 was underpinned by record performance in North America, and the company is executing a decisive shift in capital allocation, reducing future investments in Brazil while maintaining and expanding in the U.S. Key projects in mining and flat steel will drive incremental EBITDA as they ramp up, and capital returns to shareholders remain a priority through buybacks and dividends. Strategic cost management and a strong North American outlook offset ongoing pressures in Brazil, setting a new direction for the company’s growth and investment focus beyond 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Imperial Oil plans to accelerate share repurchases and targets higher production amid downstream milestone
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Imperial Oil Limited (IMO) Q2 2025

Management View

  • John R. Whelan, Chairman, President & CEO, opened the call stating he was pleased to deliver strong results for the second quarter, highlighting: "We generated cash flow from operations of nearly $1.5 billion and ended the quarter with approximately $2.4 billion of cash on hand, and we achieved these results while successfully completing significant planned turnaround activity across our integrated portfolio."
  • Whelan emphasized key milestones, including the completion of work at Kearl that doubles the turnaround interval for one of its trains, now scheduled for 2029, and the start of first production at the Strathcona renewable diesel facility in July.
  • Whelan also announced: "We’ve chosen to accelerate share repurchases through our NCIB once again and plan to complete the program by year-end."
  • Daniel E. Lyons, Senior VP of Finance & Administration, stated: "We reported net income of $949 million, down $184 million from the second quarter of 2024, primarily driven by lower upstream realizations, partly offset by higher production volumes."

Outlook

  • Whelan reported encouraging dialogue with the federal government on potential industry support for major projects, noting it is early but initial conversations have been positive.
  • No changes to the company’s guidance range for annual capital expenditures ($1.9 billion to $2.1 billion) were disclosed, with management reiterating that current trends reflect project timing and that spending is expected to increase in the second half of the year.
  • The plan to accelerate and complete the NCIB share repurchase program by year-end was reaffirmed.

Financial Results

  • The company reported net income of $949 million for the quarter and nearly $1.5 billion in cash from operating activities, ending with $2.4 billion of cash on hand.
  • Cash flows from operating activities, excluding working capital effects, were $1.413 billion, down $95 million from the prior year’s second quarter.
  • Capital expenditures totaled $473 million in Q2, $11 million higher than Q2 2024, with upstream spending largely focused on sustaining capital at Kearl, Syncrude, and Cold Lake, and downstream expenditures primarily related to the renewable diesel project.
  • Dividends paid in the quarter amounted to $367 million, with a third-quarter dividend of $0.72 per share announced.
  • Upstream production averaged 427,000 oil equivalent barrels per day, the highest second quarter in over 30 years, including a record 275,000 barrels per day at Kearl and 145,000 barrels per day at Cold Lake.
  • Kearl’s unit cash costs were reported at USD 18.86 per barrel, a decrease of nearly USD 2 per barrel compared to the first quarter.
  • Downstream refined an average of 376,000 barrels per day with 87% utilization.
  • Petroleum product sales rose to 480,000 barrels per day, supported by the Trans Mountain pipeline expansion.
  • The Chemical segment earned $21 million, down $10 million sequentially and $44 million year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Manav Gupta, UBS: Asked about the rationale for accelerating the NCIB and confidence in completing it without leveraging the balance sheet. Whelan responded: "We are very comfortable and confident...we are very comfortable in accelerating the NCIB and comfortable that we will do that without leveraging our balance sheet."
  • Gupta also asked about the durability of Imperial’s outperformance. Whelan cited the company’s "strategy to win" and unique competitive advantages, stating, "We are very confident that the performance we've had is on the basis of that strategy and the teams and the technologies and our competitive advantages."
  • Dennis Fong, CIBC: Inquired about insights from the autonomous haul system and its impact. Whelan discussed the broader technology approach and cost reductions, adding that further optimization is planned.
  • Fong also asked about improvements at Kearl. Whelan highlighted recovery, productivity, and reliability as key levers, with upgrades to hydrotransport lines and turnaround efficiencies supporting longer intervals.
  • Greg M. Pardy, RBC: Sought clarification on renewable diesel production seasonality and unplanned downtime. Whelan, joined by Scott Maloney (VP, Downstream), confirmed year-round operation and proprietary technology enabling low-temperature blending. Whelan noted unplanned downtime was minor and behind them.
  • Menno Hulshof, TD Cowen: Asked about lower CapEx and timing for EBRT pilot well activation. Whelan and Lyons attributed CapEx variance to timing, reiterating guidance, and set early 2027 for EBRT start-up.
  • Patrick O'Rourke, ATB Capital: Asked about refined product sales growth and margin impacts from Trans Mountain. Whelan and Maloney explained sales were driven by flexibility and demand, with margin-positive moves, primarily into the domestic market. O'Rourke also queried Kearl outperformance, with management attributing it to turnaround and reliability gains.
  • Douglas George Blyth Leggate, Wolfe Research: Focused on dividend policy versus buybacks and leverage. Whelan and Lyons reiterated a disciplined approach, prioritizing a reliable and growing dividend and maintaining a $4 billion gross debt level.
  • Lydia Alexandra Gould, Goldman Sachs: Asked about upcoming SAGD projects at Cold Lake and technology competitive advantages. Whelan and Cheryl L. Gomez-Smith highlighted the success of Grand Rapids, future solvent-assisted developments, and a targeted peak of 50,000 barrels per day SA-SAGD production by 2030.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was generally positive, emphasizing operational execution, growth prospects, and capital allocation. Support for the company’s strategic direction and capital returns was apparent, with remarks such as "we completely agree with you. We believe the setup is very strong for your stock to continue to work."
  • Management maintained a confident, forward-looking tone in both prepared remarks and responses, underscoring competitive advantages, technology, and discipline. Whelan used language such as "we are very confident...we are very comfortable."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the sentiment remained positive, but the current quarter included more detailed discussion of operational milestones and capital return acceleration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s guidance language remained consistent, with no downward revisions and continued emphasis on capital allocation discipline.
  • Strategic focus has shifted from project completion to operational ramp-up and optimization, particularly at Kearl and the Strathcona renewable diesel facility.
  • Analyst interest shifted toward the durability of outperformance, dividend policy, and technology advancements, compared to last quarter’s focus on leadership transition and project milestones.
  • Kearl’s production and cost performance improved, with record second quarter output and lower unit cash costs.
  • Management’s tone remained confident, while analysts continued to express optimism and seek insight into future capital returns and operational sustainability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited market volatility and commodity price fluctuations as ongoing challenges, but pointed to a resilient business model and strong cash position as mitigating factors.
  • Higher unplanned downtime in refining was acknowledged but characterized as non-concerning and resolved.
  • Analyst questions probed sustainability of performance, CapEx timing, and technology implementation, but management responses consistently emphasized confidence and readiness.

Final Takeaway

Imperial Oil’s second quarter 2025 call highlighted strong operational performance, record upstream production, and the successful completion and start-up of the Strathcona renewable diesel facility. The company announced the acceleration of its share buyback program, reaffirmed disciplined capital allocation, and maintained guidance for capital expenditures. Management pointed to ongoing technology-driven productivity gains and a robust cash position as key drivers of confidence in delivering further shareholder returns and operational growth through the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: IMI plc reports 1H results
data.description:
• IMI plc press release (OTCPK:IMIAF): 1H Revenue of £1,091m vs £1,098m. 
  • Operating profit of £198m. 
  • Profit before tax of £190m. 
  • Basic EPS of 56.1p. 

  •  Full year guidance reconfirmed

    o  Strong momentum heading into the second half

    o  On track to deliver our fourth consecutive year of mid-single digit organic revenue growth

    o  Full year adjusted basic EPS is expected to be between 129p - 136p

data.title: B&G Foods sells the Le Sueur Brand to McCall Farms
data.description:
• B&G Foods, Inc. (NYSE:BGS) announced today that it has sold the Le Sueur brand of premium sweet peas, green beans and carrots to McCall Farms.
  • The terms of the transaction were not disclosed. The sale does not include the Le Sieur brand in Canada. 
  • B&G Foods intends to use the proceeds from the sale for general corporate purposes, including the repayment of long-term debt and the purchase of assets useful in B&G Foods’ business, and to pay taxes, fees and expenses related to the sale.
  • “Our sale of the Le Sueur brand represents continued progress in our ongoing efforts to reshape our portfolio, sharpen focus on our core brands and reduce long-term debt,” said Casey Keller, President and Chief Executive Officer of B&G Foods.
  • Source: Press Release
